explanation: Remember: "their" means belonging to them. "There" means a place. "They're" means "they are."
"There" means a place or "there is." "Their" means belonging to them. "They're" means "they are."
"Your" means belonging to you. "You're" means "you are." Try replacing it with "you are" — if it makes sense, use "you're."
"Its" means belonging to it (no apostrophe!). "It's" means "it is." Try replacing with "it is" — if it works, use "it's."
"A lot" is always two words. Think of it like "a bunch" — two separate words.
Remember: "be-CAUSE." The cause comes after "be."
"Until" has only one L at the end. No double letters needed!
Remember: "I before E" — fr-I-E-nd. Your frIEnd to the END.
"Weird" is weird — it breaks the "I before E" rule! W-E-I-R-D.
There's "finite" hiding in "definitely" — de-FINITE-ly. No A!
"Which" starts with W-H. Think "WHich one?"
"Their" — think "THE-IR" — the thing that is theirs.
"I before E except after C" — re-C-E-I-ve. The C comes first, then EI.
"Tomorrow" has two R's: to-MOR-ROW.
There's "A RAT" in "separate" — sep-A-R-A-T-e.
"Different" has three syllables: DIF-FER-ENT. Don't forget the second one!
"Really" has two L's — real-LY. It's REAL + LY.
There's a silent N hiding in there: go-VERN-ment. Think "to govern."
"Probably" has all its syllables: PROB-AB-LY. Say each one!
"When" starts with W-H, just like "where," "what," and "why."
"What" starts with W-H. All question words do: what, when, where, why, who.
"Don't" needs an apostrophe — it's short for "do not." The apostrophe replaces the missing O.
"Can't" needs an apostrophe — it's short for "cannot." The apostrophe replaces the missing letters.
"Won't" needs an apostrophe — it's short for "will not" (English is funny sometimes!).
It sounds like "could of" but it's actually "could have" (or "could've"). Same for "would have" and "should have."
It sounds like "would of" but it's actually "would have" (or "would've").
It sounds like "should of" but it's actually "should have" (or "should've").
"To" = direction (go TO school). "Too" = also or excessive (me TOO, TOO much). "Two" = the number 2.
"Then" = time (first this, THEN that). "Than" = comparison (bigger THAN).
"Are" = a verb (you ARE). "Our" = belonging to us (OUR house).
